metadata.description: Satya Promise Tracker — key promises made by Indian political leaders, linked to news evidence

promises.promise: Double farmer income by 2022
promises.source_description: Indian Express: Will double farmers' income by 2022, says PM Modi
promises.gemma_reasoning: The target deadline of 2022 has passed and farm income did not double.

evidence_articles.title: Doubling Farmers' Income: A Tale of Empty Promises, Deceit, and Propaganda
evidence_articles.rephrased: NSSO survey data showed average monthly farm household income rose only to ₹10,218 by 2018-19 from a base of ~₹8,000, translating to ~8% nominal CAGR and failing the 10.4% real annual growth target needed to double income.
evidence_articles.content: Official NSSO data confirms that farm household incomes failed to double by 2022. In 2018-19, monthly household income was ₹10,218, far below the ₹16,000+ real target required to double from the 2015-16 base of ~₹8,000.

promises.notes: The target year of 2022 has passed. Real agricultural income did not double. NSSO survey data indicates average farm income rose from ~₹8,000 in 2015-16 to ₹10,218 in 2018-19, far below the ₹16,000+ target required to double in real terms.
promises.supporting_quote: NSSO survey data showed average monthly farm household income rose only to ₹10,218 by 2018-19 from a base of ~₹8,000, translating to ~8% nominal CAGR and failing the 10.4% real annual growth target needed to double income.
promises.importance_reason: Nationwide impact with a large-scale economic goal.

promises.promise: Create 2 crore jobs every year
promises.source_description: Economic Times: One crore jobs if BJP comes to power, says Narendra Modi
promises.gemma_reasoning: CMIE data showed rising unemployment post-2016, contradicting the job creation promise.

evidence_articles.title: Where are the Promised Two Crore Jobs Every Year?
evidence_articles.rephrased: CMIE data shows total employment in India fell from 41.27 crore in 2016-17 to 40.57 crore in 2022-23, representing a net loss of 70 lakh jobs instead of meeting the 2 crore annual target.
evidence_articles.content: According to CMIE reports, instead of adding 2 crore jobs annually, total employment saw a net contraction from 41.27 crore (2016-17) to 40.57 crore (2022-23) amid growing numbers of new job seekers.

promises.notes: CMIE statistics show that total employment contracted from 41.27 crore in 2016-17 to 40.57 crore in 2022-23, indicating a net loss of jobs over this period instead of meeting the 2 crore annual target.
promises.supporting_quote: CMIE data shows total employment in India fell from 41.27 crore in 2016-17 to 40.57 crore in 2022-23, representing a net loss of 70 lakh jobs instead of meeting the 2 crore annual target.
promises.importance_reason: deterministic force critical (scale signal)

promises.promise: Bring back black money from Swiss banks and deposit Rs 15 lakh in every Indian's account
promises.source_description: BOOM Live: Did Modi Promise To Deposit Rs 15 Lakh In Every Account? A Factcheck
promises.gemma_reasoning: No black money repatriation happened, and the Rs 15 lakh promise was called a 'jumla'.

evidence_articles.title: Indian money in Swiss banks dips to CHF 3.25 bn; customer deposits up
evidence_articles.rephrased: Despite years of government efforts (Black Money Act, demonetisation, tax information exchange), significant Indian funds continue to remain in Swiss banks, with customer deposits even rising 50% in 2025. Direct benefit of ₹15 lakh was never credited to citizens' accounts.
evidence_articles.content: Significant Indian funds remain in Swiss banks with customer deposits rising in 2025, showing that bringing back huge sums and distributing them has not materialized. Direct benefit of ₹15 lakh was never credited to citizens' accounts.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Despite years of government efforts, significant Indian funds continue to remain in Swiss banks... and no ₹15 lakh (or even a small fraction) was ever credited to citizens' accounts.

promises.notes: Limited Recovery Impact: Despite years of government efforts (Black Money Act, demonetisation, tax information exchange), significant Indian funds continue to remain in Swiss banks (with customer deposits even rising 50% in 2025), showing the promise of bringing back huge sums and distributing them has not materialised. No Direct Benefit Delivered: The article confirms the amounts are negligible compared to campaign rhetoric, and no ₹15 lakh (or even a small fraction) was ever credited to citizens’ accounts, reinforcing the view that the high-decibel assurance remains unfulfilled.
promises.supporting_quote: Despite years of government efforts, significant Indian funds continue to remain in Swiss banks... and no ₹15 lakh (or even a small fraction) was ever credited to citizens' accounts.
promises.importance_reason: deterministic force critical (scale signal)

promises.promise: Build 2 crore pucca houses for the poor by 2022 (PM Awas Yojana)
promises.source_description: PMAY-Urban: Official Scheme Page and 'Housing for All' Guidelines
promises.gemma_reasoning: The target was extended and cumulative completions eventually crossed 4 crore, exceeding the original target of 2 crore.

evidence_articles.title: PMAY Housing for All: Targets Missed and Deadlines Extended
evidence_articles.rephrased: By early 2023, PMAY-Gramin completed ~2.1 crore houses out of 2.94 crore target (~72%), and PMAY-Urban completed only ~61 lakh houses out of 1.25 crore sanctioned (~51%), meaning the original March 2022 deadline was missed.
evidence_articles.content: By early 2023, about 72% of the rural target (2.1 crore out of 2.94 crore) and 51% of the urban target (61 lakh out of 1.25 crore) was completed, showing the 2022 deadline was missed with many houses incomplete.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Rural (PMAY-G): ~2.1 crore houses completed out of ~2.94 crore target (~72%). Urban (PMAY-U): ~61 lakh completed out of ~1.25 crore sanctioned (~51%).

evidence_articles.title: PMAY-U Official Extension Guidelines and Scheme Status
evidence_articles.rephrased: The government officially extended PMAY-Urban to December 2024 to complete already sanctioned houses, and extended PMAY-Gramin with updated timelines.
evidence_articles.content: Due to unmet targets by the 2022 deadline, PMAY-Urban was extended to December 2024/2025, and PMAY-Gramin was also extended to 2024.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The government officially extended both components: PMAY-U extended till Dec 2024/2025 for completing already sanctioned houses.

evidence_articles.title: Massive Success of Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana
evidence_articles.rephrased: Cumulative completions under PMAY (Urban + Rural) crossed 4 crore by 2025-26, with the government announcing 2 crore additional houses for the next phase.
evidence_articles.content: Under PMAY, cumulative housing completions crossed 4 crore in 2025-26. The government highlights this as a major achievement with a new phase of 2 crore houses planned.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Total houses completed under PMAY (Urban + Rural) have crossed 4 crore cumulatively. Rural alone: Over 2.7–2.99 crore completed.

promises.notes: Summary: The specific "by 2022" target was not fully achieved on time (hence extended), but the scheme has delivered far beyond the original 2 crore mark over a longer period. Critics call it a broken deadline promise; the government highlights it as a major achievement with continued momentum.
promises.supporting_quote: Rural (PMAY-G): ~2.1 crore houses completed out of ~2.94 crore target (~72%). Urban (PMAY-U): ~61 lakh completed out of ~1.25 crore sanctioned (~51%).
promises.importance_reason: deterministic force critical (scale signal)

promises.promise: Make India a $5 trillion economy by 2024-25
promises.source_description: Times of India: Indian economy to reach 5 trillion size by 2022, says PM Modi
promises.gemma_reasoning: Target deadline of 2024 passed, and the $5 trillion GDP goal was not achieved.

evidence_articles.title: Five Trillion Dollar Economy: How Modi and India Failed Again
evidence_articles.rephrased: India's nominal GDP for FY 2024-25 stood at around $3.9 trillion, and is estimated at approximately $4.0–4.15 trillion for FY 2025-26, missing the $5 trillion target by the 2024-25 deadline.
evidence_articles.content: As of FY 2024-25, India's nominal GDP is around $3.9 trillion, and FY 2025-26 estimates are around $4.0–4.15 trillion, indicating that the target of reaching a $5 trillion economy by 2024-25 has not been achieved.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: India's nominal GDP for FY 2024-25: Around $3.9 trillion. For FY 2025-26: Estimated at approximately $4.0–4.15 trillion.

promises.notes: India missed the target of reaching a $5 trillion GDP by 2024-25. Nominal GDP for FY 2024-25 was around $3.9 trillion, and FY 2025-26 estimates place it around $4.0–4.15 trillion (with IMF and official stats placing it in this range), failing to meet the target timeline.
promises.supporting_quote: India's nominal GDP for FY 2024-25: Around $3.9 trillion. For FY 2025-26: Estimated at approximately $4.0–4.15 trillion.
promises.importance_reason: deterministic force critical (scale signal)

promises.promise: End corruption completely — 'Na khaunga, na khane dunga'
promises.source_description: Aaj Tak: PM Modi warns corrupt officials with anti-corruption slogans
promises.gemma_reasoning: Multiple corruption scandals occurred during Modi's tenure.

evidence_articles.title: India's electoral bonds: 'Laundry' where corrupt firms paid, got cleansed
evidence_articles.rephrased: The electoral bonds scheme enabled companies under investigation to buy anonymity and influence by donating hundreds of crores to political parties (e.g. BJP) while receiving relief from probes or securing public contracts, contradicting anti-corruption pledges.
evidence_articles.content: Electoral bonds allowed anonymous corporate donations, which investigations showed were used by several firms facing tax and ED raids to donate to the ruling party, after which probes softened or contracts were awarded.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Quid Pro Quo Pattern: Several companies facing ED and IT raids donated via electoral bonds to BJP, after which probes softened or they received public contracts.

promises.notes: Quid Pro Quo Pattern: The article reveals that several companies facing Enforcement Directorate and Income Tax raids (e.g., Aurobindo Pharma, Megha Engineering) donated hundreds of crores via electoral bonds to the BJP, after which probes softened or they received government praise/contracts. Opacity Enabled Corruption: Introduced by the Modi government in 2017, the electoral bonds scheme allowed anonymous donations (totaling over ₹6,000 crore to BJP alone), which the Supreme Court later struck down as unconstitutional for enabling unchecked influence-peddling. Failure of Anti-Corruption Pledge: Companies under investigation 'got cleansed' by donating to ruling parties, directly contradicting the anti-corruption slogan.
promises.supporting_quote: Quid Pro Quo Pattern: Several companies facing ED and IT raids donated via electoral bonds to BJP, after which probes softened or they received public contracts.
promises.importance_reason: A promise to end corruption completely is a nation-shaping goal with vast implications for governance and public trust.

promises.promise: Electoral bonds will bring transparency to political funding
promises.source_description: Indian Express: Electoral bonds funding elections controversy in Parliament
promises.gemma_reasoning: Electoral bonds were struck down as unconstitutional in Feb 2024.

evidence_articles.title: Supreme Court of India Judgement on Electoral Bonds Scheme - 15-Feb-2024
evidence_articles.rephrased: The Supreme Court of India declared the Electoral Bonds scheme unconstitutional, noting that anonymous political funding violates the right to information and fails to bring transparency.
evidence_articles.content: The five-judge Constitution bench struck down the Electoral Bonds scheme as unconstitutional because hiding donor identities from the public violates Article 19(1)(a)'s guarantee of the right to information.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The claim that electoral bonds brought transparency is false; the Supreme Court struck down the scheme, and reporting described it as opaque because donor identities were hidden from the public.

promises.notes: The claim that electoral bonds brought transparency is false; the Supreme Court struck down the scheme in February 2024, declaring it unconstitutional. Donor identities were hidden from the public, making the system highly opaque and violating the right to information under Article 19(1)(a).
promises.supporting_quote: The claim that electoral bonds brought transparency is false; the Supreme Court struck down the scheme, and reporting described it as opaque because donor identities were hidden from the public.
promises.importance_reason: A nationwide change to political funding with potential to reshape campaign finance.

promises.promise: Har ghar nal se jal — piped water to every household by 2024
promises.source_description: Hindustan Times: Piped water to be Modi 2.0 priority
promises.gemma_reasoning: Target deadline of 2024 has passed and the promise is likely to be missed.

evidence_articles.title: No Har Ghar Jal by 2024
evidence_articles.rephrased: Reports confirmed that the Jal Jeevan Mission would miss its 2024 target of 100% piped water coverage across rural households.
evidence_articles.content: Analysis of the scheme shows that implementation challenges and slow progress in several key states made the 2024 target impossible to achieve on time.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The central government’s own update said that as of 30 Jan 2024, only 14.21 crore out of 19.27 crore rural households had tap water, so the 2024 target was not yet met.

evidence_articles.title: Jal Jeevan Mission Progress Update - Jan 2024
evidence_articles.rephrased: As of January 30, 2024, only 14.21 crore out of 19.27 crore rural households had tap water connections, meaning the target was not met on time.
evidence_articles.content: Official government data released in early 2024 confirmed that approximately 5 crore rural households were still without tap water connections.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Only 14.21 crore out of 19.27 crore rural households had tap water.

evidence_articles.title: Jal Jeevan Mission falling short of 2024 target
evidence_articles.rephrased: The mission was reported to be falling short of its 2024 target due to persistent delays and state-level implementation deficits.
evidence_articles.content: Independent assessments and reporting confirmed that the scheme's pace was insufficient to meet the target of 100% coverage by the end of 2024.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The mission was likely to fall short of its 2024 target.

evidence_articles.title: Har Ghar Jal mission misses 2024 target; nearly half of households in 4 states left out
evidence_articles.rephrased: The scheme officially missed its December 2024 deadline, with nearly half of the households in four major states remaining without piped water.
evidence_articles.content: Reporting after the deadline showed that the mission missed its 2024 target, leaving large portions of the rural population without access to tap water in several lagging states.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The scheme missed its December 2024 deadline.

promises.notes: The central government’s own update said that as of 30 Jan 2024, only 14.21 crore out of 19.27 crore rural households had tap water, so the 2024 target was not yet met. The Hindu reported that the mission was likely to fall short of its 2024 target, and Moneycontrol later confirmed that the scheme missed its December 2024 deadline, leaving nearly half of households in four states left out.
promises.supporting_quote: The central government’s own update said that as of 30 Jan 2024, only 14.21 crore out of 19.27 crore rural households had tap water, so the 2024 target was not yet met.
promises.importance_reason: deterministic force critical (scale signal)

promises.promise: Uniform Civil Code implementation
promises.source_description: Hindustan Times: BJP promises Uniform Civil Code, CAA in Lok Sabha elections manifesto
promises.gemma_reasoning: National UCC not yet implemented.

evidence_articles.title: Uttarakhand becomes first state to implement Uniform Civil Code
evidence_articles.rephrased: Uttarakhand became the first state in independent India to enact and implement a UCC (passed Feb 2024, Presidential assent March 2024, formally rolled out Jan 2025). The code covers marriage, divorce, inheritance, and live-in relationships with exemptions for tribals.
evidence_articles.content: The Uttarakhand Assembly passed the UCC bill, making it the first state to implement a common law governing personal issues like marriage, live-in relations, and inheritance.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Uttarakhand became the first state in independent India to enact and implement a UCC (passed Feb 2024, Presidential assent March 2024, formally rolled out Jan 2025; amendments in 2026). It covers marriage, divorce, inheritance, live-in relationships (mandatory registration), bans polygamy/triple talaq etc., with exemptions for tribals.

promises.notes: Uttarakhand became the first state in independent India to enact and implement a UCC (passed Feb 2024, Presidential assent March 2024, formally rolled out Jan 2025; amendments in 2026). It covers marriage, divorce, inheritance, live-in relationships (mandatory registration), bans polygamy/triple talaq etc., with exemptions for tribals. Other BJP-ruled states are drafting UCC bills, but no national law has been enacted yet.
promises.supporting_quote: Uttarakhand became the first state in independent India to enact and implement a UCC (passed Feb 2024, Presidential assent March 2024, formally rolled out Jan 2025; amendments in 2026). It covers marriage, divorce, inheritance, live-in relationships (mandatory registration), bans polygamy/triple talaq etc., with exemptions for tribals.
promises.importance_reason: A Uniform Civil Code is a nation-wide, transformative legal change with significant social and political implications.

promises.promise: One nation one election
promises.source_description: Scroll.in: BJP manifesto promises One Nation One Election, Uniform Civil Code
promises.gemma_reasoning: Bill introduced, under examination by a Joint Parliamentary Committee.

evidence_articles.title: Govt targeting 2034 to hold 1st One Nation One Election
evidence_articles.rephrased: The Constitution 129th Amendment Bill & Union Territories Bill for simultaneous elections were introduced in December 2024 and sent to a Joint Parliamentary Committee. Full implementation requires constitutional amendments, with the target for the first joint polls set for 2034.
evidence_articles.content: Simultaneous elections bills were introduced in December 2024 and referred to a JPC. The government targets 2034 for holding the first simultaneous elections, requiring a two-thirds majority constitutional amendment.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Bills (Constitution 129th Amendment Bill & Union Territories Bill) were introduced in Lok Sabha in December 2024. They are being examined by a JPC. Government targets first simultaneous elections by 2034.

promises.notes: Status: Promise in progress (Bills introduced, under parliamentary scrutiny; not yet implemented). Bills (Constitution 129th Amendment Bill & Union Territories Bill) were introduced in Lok Sabha in December 2024. They are being examined by a Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC), whose tenure has been extended. Government targets first simultaneous elections by 2034, with partial alignment possibly from 2029. Full implementation requires a constitutional amendment (two-thirds majority).
promises.supporting_quote: Bills (Constitution 129th Amendment Bill & Union Territories Bill) were introduced in Lok Sabha in December 2024. They are being examined by a JPC. Government targets first simultaneous elections by 2034.
promises.importance_reason: A nationwide electoral reform of this scale would be transformative for Indian democracy.

promises.promise: Demonetization will end black money and terrorism funding
promises.source_description: Indian Express: Narendra Modi Prime Minister address to the nation on Demonetization
promises.gemma_reasoning: Demonetization failed to end black money and terrorism funding.

evidence_articles.title: 99.3% of demonetised currency returned to banks: RBI
evidence_articles.rephrased: The RBI confirmed that 99.3% of the demonetized currency (₹15.3 lakh crore) returned to the banking system, indicating minimal seizure of black money in cash form.
evidence_articles.content: According to the Reserve Bank of India, out of ₹15.41 lakh crore in demonetized currency, ₹15.31 lakh crore was returned, leaving only ₹10,720 crore outside the banking system.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Over 99.3% of the demonetised currency (₹15.3 lakh crore) returned to the banking system, indicating minimal seizure of black money in cash form.

promises.notes: Over 99.3% of the demonetised currency (₹15.3 lakh crore) returned to the banking system, indicating minimal seizure of black money in cash form. Black money generation and circulation continued through other means; counterfeit currency detections persisted (with rises in some years). Terrorism funding was disrupted short-term but not ended.
promises.supporting_quote: Over 99.3% of the demonetised currency (₹15.3 lakh crore) returned to the banking system, indicating minimal seizure of black money in cash form.
promises.importance_reason: Aims to fundamentally change the Indian economy by eliminating black money.

promises.promise: Bullet train between Mumbai and Ahmedabad by 2023
promises.source_description: Hindustan Times: Mostly elevated, partly under sea: All you need to know about Mumbai-Ahmedabad bullet train
promises.gemma_reasoning: Target deadline of 2023 passed, project delayed to 2027-2028.

evidence_articles.title: India's bullet train project nine years later: Is the dream finally nearing reality?
evidence_articles.rephrased: The project missed its 2023 deadline due to land acquisition and COVID-19 delays. Phased services starting with the Surat-Bilimora stretch are targeted for August 2027, with full corridor completion by 2029.
evidence_articles.content: Nine years after launch, the Mumbai-Ahmedabad bullet train corridor is progressing, but it missed its original 2023 target. Phased operations on a short stretch are slated for 2027, and full corridor completion is expected by 2029.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The project missed the 2023 deadline due to land acquisition issues (especially in Maharashtra), COVID-19, and other challenges.

promises.notes: The Mumbai-Ahmedabad Bullet Train (508 km) is India's first high-speed rail project (designed for 320 kmph), promising to reduce travel time to about 2 hours. Launched with much fanfare in 2017 with a 2023 deadline and Japanese collaboration, it faced major delays due to land acquisition issues (especially in Maharashtra) and COVID-19. While not operational by the promised 2023 date, phased services (Surat-Bilimora) are targeted to begin in August 2027, with the full corridor expected by 2029.
promises.supporting_quote: The project missed the 2023 deadline due to land acquisition issues (especially in Maharashtra), COVID-19, and other challenges.
promises.importance_reason: Nationally significant infrastructure project with large-scale impact.

promises.promise: Remove Rohingyas from India completely
promises.source_description: The Hindu: Rohingya are illegal immigrants who need to be deported, says Kiren Rijiju
promises.gemma_reasoning: The government has not fully deported the Rohingya population, with thousands still residing in India.

evidence_articles.title: UN probes reports of Rohingya refugees cast into sea from Indian naval vessel; SC says fanciful claims
evidence_articles.rephrased: An estimated ~40,000 Rohingyas remain in India (in slums, camps, and detentions) despite crackdowns and some deportations, showing that complete removal has not been achieved.
evidence_articles.content: Reporting shows that ~40,000 Rohingyas remain in India. Over 20,000 are UNHCR-registered. Raids and detentions have not resulted in total deportation due to legal challenges.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: An estimated ~40,000 Rohingyas still live in India.

evidence_articles.title: India and the Rohingya deportation issue
evidence_articles.rephrased: Despite government attempts to deport all illegal Rohingya immigrants, legal hurdles and the large scale have kept tens of thousands inside the country.
evidence_articles.content: Frontline analyzes the Rohingya deportation process, noting that full deportation has run into legal and international challenges, with the majority remaining in camps.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Full removal has not been achieved due to legal challenges and scale.

promises.notes: The BJP government has consistently classified Rohingya Muslims from Myanmar as illegal immigrants and a security risk, vowing their full identification, detention, and complete deportation (India not a signatory to the Refugee Convention). While 2025 saw stronger enforcement with some expulsions, tens of thousands remain in the country (estimated ~40,000) amid Supreme Court cases, practical hurdles, and humanitarian issues. The promise of total removal stands unfulfilled.
promises.supporting_quote: An estimated ~40,000 Rohingyas still live in India.
promises.importance_reason: Nation-wide policy with potentially large-scale impact on immigration and demographics.

promises.promise: Free and world class government schools in Delhi
promises.source_description: Times of India: Several firsts in education, health, power: Kejriwal
promises.gemma_reasoning: Delhi government school results improved significantly, fulfilling the promise.

evidence_articles.title: Why Delhi govt schools again beat private institutions in CBSE board results
evidence_articles.rephrased: Delhi government schools consistently outperformed private schools in board exam results, with pass percentages above 97-98% in recent years under the reform programs.
evidence_articles.content: Reporting shows that Delhi government schools beat private schools in board exams, reflecting the academic gains from infrastructure and teaching quality reforms.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Delhi government schools beat private institutions in CBSE board results.

evidence_articles.title: Atishi turns spotlight on 'world-class' govt schools in Delhi
evidence_articles.rephrased: The AAP government highlighted the construction of over 22,000 new classrooms, smart classrooms, labs, and modern infrastructure as part of the education transformation.
evidence_articles.content: Delhi's education department highlighted infrastructure upgrades, international teacher training programs, and CBSE board pass rates as evidence of successful government school reforms.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Delhi government schools underwent a major transformation under AAP: over 22,000 new classrooms built.

promises.notes: Arvind Kejriwal’s AAP government made education a top priority, allocating the highest share of the budget to transform Delhi’s government schools into free, modern institutions with world-class infrastructure, innovative curricula, and improved learning outcomes. The reforms delivered visible results — better buildings, facilities, teacher development, and strong academic performance (pass percentages above 97-98%) — making government schools a preferred choice for many parents.
promises.supporting_quote: Delhi government schools beat private institutions in CBSE board results; over 22,000 new classrooms built.
promises.importance_reason: Transformational commitment affecting millions of Delhi residents and setting a national standard.

promises.promise: Free electricity up to 200 units in Delhi
promises.source_description: Indian Express: No bill for Delhi consumers using up to 200 units electricity: Arvind Kejriwal
promises.gemma_reasoning: Free electricity up to 200 units has been implemented and sustained in Delhi.

evidence_articles.title: More people availed electricity subsidy under BJP compared to AAP in the same period last year: Govt data
evidence_articles.rephrased: Government data showed that the electricity subsidy scheme continues to be widely availed by millions of Delhi households, remaining fully active under new administrations.
evidence_articles.content: Official data confirmed that the electricity subsidy scheme was availed by a higher number of consumers during late 2025, showing it remains fully operational.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The scheme was implemented in 2019 and has been continued, remaining active even in 2025-26.

evidence_articles.title: Over 93% households benefitted from power subsidy in Dec 2025
evidence_articles.rephrased: Over 93% of the target domestic electricity consumers in Delhi benefited from the power subsidy in December 2025.
evidence_articles.content: Reports confirmed that the power subsidy scheme resulted in zero bills or discounted bills for the vast majority of households, showing continued operations into 2026.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Over 93% households benefitted from power subsidy in Dec 2025.

promises.notes: Arvind Kejriwal’s AAP government fulfilled its promise by providing free electricity up to 200 units per month to Delhi households since 2019. The subsidy scheme has been regularly extended, delivering zero bills to a large section of residents (especially lower and middle-income families) and remains operational, significantly reducing power costs for consumers.
promises.supporting_quote: Arvind Kejriwal’s AAP government fulfilled its promise by providing free electricity up to 200 units per month to Delhi households since 2019.
promises.importance_reason: Direct financial impact on millions of households.

promises.promise: Clean Yamuna river by 2025
promises.source_description: The Hindu: Delhi govt pledges to clean Yamuna by 2025
promises.gemma_reasoning: The target deadline of 2025 has passed.

evidence_articles.title: Delhi waiting for 'world-famous' dip: Amit Shah slams Arvind Kejriwal over Yamuna pollution
evidence_articles.rephrased: The 2025 deadline passed without the river being cleaned. Pollution levels remained high with untreated sewage and toxic foam continuing to appear, drawing public and political criticism.
evidence_articles.content: Reports from early 2025 showed that the target to clean the Yamuna by 2025 was missed, with political leaders pointing out that the river is still heavily polluted and citizens are unable to take the promised dip.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: The 2025 deadline passed without the river being cleaned. Toxic foam continued to appear on the Yamuna in Delhi during 2024-2025.

promises.notes: Arvind Kejriwal’s AAP government pledged to make the Yamuna pollution-free by February 2025 through sewage treatment upgrades, drain cleaning, and industrial checks. Despite spending thousands of crores, the target was not met — the river remained severely polluted with ongoing issues of untreated sewage and toxic foam as the deadline passed. Cleaning efforts are a long-term challenge involving multiple states.
promises.supporting_quote: The 2025 deadline passed without the river being cleaned. Toxic foam continued to appear on the Yamuna in Delhi during 2024-2025.
promises.importance_reason: Direct impact on the environment and public health in Delhi.

promises.promise: Guarantee minimum income of Rs 72,000 per year to poorest families (NYAY scheme)
promises.source_description: NDTV: Rahul Gandhi says 20% of poorest families will get Rs 72,000 per year as part of Congress's minimum income guarantee
promises.gemma_reasoning: Congress lost the 2019 election, so the NYAY scheme was not implemented.

evidence_articles.title: Nyuntam Aay Yojana - Wikipedia
evidence_articles.rephrased: The NYAY scheme was a flagship minimum income guarantee pledge made by the Congress party in the 2019 Lok Sabha elections, but it was never implemented as the party did not come to power.
evidence_articles.content: NYAY (Nyuntam Aay Yojana) was a proposed social welfare program promising ₹72,000 per year to the poorest 20% of families. The program was not implemented following the 2019 general election results.
evidence_articles.supporting_quote: Since Congress did not win the 2019 elections and form the government, the promise could not be fulfilled and is marked void.

promises.notes: Rahul Gandhi announced NYAY as Congress’s ambitious anti-poverty programme in 2019, promising direct cash transfer of ₹72,000 annually to the poorest 20% of Indian families (benefiting around 25 crore people) to eliminate extreme poverty. Since Congress did not win the 2019 elections and form the government, the promise could not be fulfilled and is marked void. A similar but different income support idea (Mahalakshmi scheme) appeared in the 2024 manifesto but was also not implemented nationally.
promises.supporting_quote: Since Congress did not win the 2019 elections and form the government, the promise could not be fulfilled and is marked void.
promises.importance_reason: Nationwide impact with potential to significantly alter economic landscape.

promises.promise: End mafia raj and crime completely in Uttar Pradesh
promises.source_description: Times of India: TOI Dialogues: How Yogi government ended mafiaraj in Uttar Pradesh's Gorakhpur
promises.gemma_reasoning: The government successfully dismantled mafia networks and reduced crime rates according to NCRB data.

evidence_articles.title: NCRB data testifies to U.P.’s strong law and order: DGP
evidence_articles.rephrased: Official NCRB 2024 data showed a significant decline in crime rates in UP across key categories, supporting the state's claims of a strong zero-tolerance law and order environment.
